title: Symbolic Computation Group at the University of Waterloo: The Team Behind Maple
overview: The Symbolic Computation Group at the University of Waterloo built Maple as a practical computer algebra system and language environment. It represents the academic-to-commercial path by which symbolic mathematics became a durable software platform.
key_contributions: **Maple (1980):** Built a symbolic computation environment that made algebraic manipulation and mathematical programming accessible on smaller machines.
**Computer algebra tooling:** Helped establish symbolic mathematics as a usable software domain outside a few elite research labs.
pivotal_people: **Keith Geddes, Gaston Gonnet, and collaborators:** Drove the computer-algebra work that became Maple.
legacy: The group matters because it turned symbolic computation into a widely distributed software product rather than a one-off research artifact. Maple helped preserve a line of language and system design where mathematics, algebraic rewriting, and interactive exploration remain central.
ai_assisted_discovery_missions: 1. "Compare Waterloo's Maple effort with MIT Project MAC's MACSYMA to show two different institutional paths for computer algebra."
